skill.name: ui-ux-polish
skill.description: Iterative UI and UX polishing workflow for improving web applications after the core product already works.
skill.summary: Use repeated, focused design passes to improve visual quality, interaction details, and responsive behavior.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-cryptography and Ruby backtick patterns, but the cited lines are markdown prose, prompt examples, or fenced blocks in SKILL.md. No malicious intent, network access, secret access, executable scripts, or prompt-injection attempt was found. The only retained risk indicator is a documented local `bd create` command example for task tracking.

low_findings.title: False Positive Static Findings in Markdown Content
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and Ruby backtick findings occur in markdown front matter, prose, prompt examples, and fenced blocks. I found no cryptographic API usage, dynamic code execution, data exfiltration, or malicious instruction in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visible markdown text and prompt reference material, not executable code. The weak-cryptography hits do not correspond to actual cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Benign Local Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes example `bd create` commands for creating UI polish tasks in a Beads workflow. The commands are hardcoded documentation examples and do not run automatically, but they still indicate a local external-command workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command block is explicit and uses a local CLI, so the external-command indicator is real. Risk is low because arguments are hardcoded examples and there is no automatic execution path.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The reported system reconnaissance location discusses using file reservations to avoid multi-agent conflicts. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment probing, credential discovery, or filesystem reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location is plain workflow guidance for coordination between agents. It contains no reconnaissance command or request for sensitive local information.

content.value_statement: Good web apps often still feel unfinished on desktop or mobile. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a repeatable workflow for improving polish through focused design passes.
actual_capabilities: Provides a reusable prompt for iterative UI and UX improvement.
Guides separate review of desktop and mobile experiences.
Identifies common polish targets such as spacing, typography, contrast, and states.
Suggests repeated improvement passes instead of one large redesign.
Includes a simple workflow for coordinating multiple agents on UI tasks.
Offers task examples for organizing polish work with Beads.
limitations: It does not replace user research, analytics, or accessibility testing.
It assumes the application already works and has basic styling.
It may produce subjective design changes that need human review.
It does not provide framework-specific component code.

use_cases.title: Improve a Product Dashboard
use_cases.description: Run focused polish passes on layout density, navigation, states, and responsive behavior before release.

use_cases.title: Review a Marketing App Experience
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to raise visual quality across landing pages, forms, and mobile views.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Agent Design Tasks
use_cases.description: Break UI polish into desktop and mobile work items so multiple agents can improve separate areas.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Polish Pass
prompt_templates.scenario: Use after the app works and needs visible refinement.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this web app and improve its UI and UX polish. Focus on spacing, typography, contrast, controls, and responsive behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Desktop and Mobile Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when both desktop and mobile need separate attention.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate the desktop and mobile experiences separately. Make targeted UI and UX improvements that fit each screen size and interaction model.

prompt_templates.title: Interaction State Polish
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when the interface feels static or unfinished.
prompt_templates.prompt: Improve the interaction details across this app. Review hover, focus, loading, empty, disabled, error, and success states.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Iterative Refinement
prompt_templates.scenario: Use after earlier passes when only subtle quality gaps remain.
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform another high-standard UI and UX polish pass. Look for small issues that reduce clarity, premium feel, accessibility, or mobile usability.

output_examples.input: A dashboard works but feels visually uneven.
output: Tighter spacing rhythm across panels and toolbars.
Clearer hierarchy for metrics, labels, and secondary actions.
Improved hover, focus, loading, and empty states.

output_examples.input: A mobile view feels cramped compared with desktop.
output: Larger touch targets and simplified navigation.
Adjusted breakpoints and stacked content order.
Reduced visual noise while preserving key actions.

output_examples.input: A team wants several agents to polish a product area.
output: Separate work items for desktop and mobile views.
Focused review areas that avoid overlapping edits.
A repeatable process for comparing each pass.

best_practices: Run the workflow only after core functionality and basic layout are stable.
Review every generated change for usability, accessibility, and regressions.
Test desktop and mobile views separately after each polish pass.
anti_patterns: Using polish passes to hide broken flows or incomplete product logic.
Accepting subjective visual changes without checking real screens.
Running many agents on the same files without coordination.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It provides a repeatable prompt and workflow for improving UI and UX polish in existing web applications.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it after the app works, basic styling exists, and the remaining work is refinement.

faq.question: Does it generate a full design system?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on iterative polish, not creating a design system from scratch.

faq.question: Can it help with mobile UX?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow explicitly asks the model to evaluate desktop and mobile experiences separately.

faq.question: Does it run commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill is markdown guidance. It includes optional example commands for local task tracking.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: It is listed for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
